categories: Meta Ranking (MR): a novel cross-query-comparison-based method that assesses reliability by pairwisely ranking the target query-response pair with multiple reference query-response pairs. This approach is designed to enable weak LLMs to effectively evaluate the reliability of LLM responses., Model cascading: a technique where open- and closed-source LLMs are combined to achieve performance comparable to GPT-4-turbo with lower costs. This method is demonstrated as an application of Meta Ranking., Instruction tuning: the use of Meta Ranking for iterative training data filtering, which significantly reduces data processing time and enables LLaMA-7B and Phi-2 to surpass Alpaca-13B with fewer training tokens.

title: The development of a general purpose service robot for daily life necessitates the robot's ability...
abstract: The paper presents a task planning method based on a constrained LLM prompt scheme that generates an executable action sequence from a command. An exceptional handling module is proposed to deal with the LLM hallucinations problem, ensuring the generated results are admissible in the current environment. The method is evaluated on the commands generated by the RoboCup@Home Command Generator and demonstrates exceptional performance in comprehending instructions and executing tasks.

title: Despite their performance, large language models (LLMs) can inadvertently perpetuate biases found in the data...
abstract: The analysis reveals that large language models can unintentionally perpetuate biases present in their training data, mirroring human biases. To mitigate this issue, the authors explore two crowd-based strategies: averaging responses from multiple LLMs and locally weighted aggregation methods. They find that the former exacerbates existing biases due to limited diversity within LLM crowds, while the latter more effectively leverages the wisdom of the LLM crowd, achieving bias mitigation and improved accuracy. Recognizing the complementary strengths of LLMs (accuracy) and humans (diversity), the authors demonstrate that hybrid crowds containing both significantly enhance performance and further reduce biases across ethnic and gender-related contexts.

title: Fine-tuning Large Language Models (LLMs) with multimodal encoders on modality-specific data expands the modalities that...
In this paper, we introduce a novel learning paradigm for Adaptive Large Language Model (LLM)...
abstract: The paper presents a novel learning paradigm for Adaptive Large Language Model (LLM) agents that allows for continual adaptation via memory-based online reinforcement learning, eliminating the need for fine-tuning and reducing reliance on computationally intensive gradient updates. The method is formalized as a Memory-augmented Markov Decision Process (M-MDP), equipped with a neural case-selection policy to guide action decisions. Past experiences are stored in an episodic memory, either differentiable or non-parametric. The model, named Memento, achieves top performance on GAIA validation ($87.88%$ Pass@$3$) and $79.40%$ on the test set, outperforming the state-of-the-art training-based method in certain tasks. The approach offers a scalable and efficient pathway for developing generalist LLM agents capable of continuous, real-time learning without gradient updates, advancing machine learning towards open-ended skill acquisition and deep research scenarios. The Memento agent model outperforms the state-of-the-art training-based method on certain tasks, adding $4.7%$ to $9.6%$ absolute points on out-of-distribution tasks. The code for the Memento agent model is available at https://github.com/Agent-on-the-Fly/Memento.

title: This paper introduces a novel Bayesian learning model to explain the behavior of Large Language...
abstract: The paper presents a new Bayesian learning model to elucidate the behavior of Large Language Models (LLMs), particularly focusing on their core optimization metric, next token prediction. A theoretical framework is developed using an ideal generative text model represented by a multinomial transition probability matrix with a prior. The study investigates how LLMs approximate this matrix. Key contributions include a continuity theorem relating embeddings to multinomial distributions, demonstration of LLM text generation aligning with Bayesian learning principles, an explanation for the emergence of in-context learning in larger models, and empirical validation using visualizations of next token probabilities from an instrumented Llama model. The findings offer new insights into LLM functioning, providing a statistical foundation for understanding their capabilities and limitations.
categories: Development of a theoretical framework based on an ideal generative text model represented by a multinomial transition probability matrix with a prior., Examination of how LLMs approximate the aforementioned matrix., Empirical validation using visualizations of next token probabilities from an instrumented Llama model.

title: The rapid advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) has led to their increased integration into...
abstract: The abstract discusses Alopex, a framework designed to improve the integration and performance of Large Language Models (LLMs) on mobile devices for personalized assistance. The main goal is to address challenges such as data scarcity, ineffective question formatting, and catastrophic forgetting that impede the development of on-device LLM agents. The proposed solution uses Alopex with the Fox LLM, introducing a logic-based method for generating high-quality training data, a novel 'description-question-output' format for fine-tuning, and a data mixing strategy to mitigate catastrophic forgetting.
categories: The use of Alopex with the Fox LLM., Logic-based method for generating high-quality training data., A novel 'description-question-output' format for fine-tuning., Data mixing strategy to mitigate catastrophic forgetting.

title: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable performance on various natural language tasks. However, they...
abstract: This work identifies a key issue: heterogeneous token overfitting (HTO), where large language models (LLMs) overfit different tokens in the provided knowledge at varying rates. To tackle this, they propose OVERTONE, a token-level smoothing method that mitigates HTO by adaptively refining the target distribution. OVERTONE proves effective and versatile across various editing methods, LLMs, and scenarios.

title: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional performance across a wide variety of domains. Nonetheless,...
abstract: The abstract discusses the Way-to-Specialist (WTS) framework, which aims to enhance the specialized capability of Large Language Models (LLMs) without domain-specific training. This is achieved through a bidirectional enhancement between LLMs and domain knowledge graphs (DKGs). The WTS framework consists of two components: DKG-Augmented LLM and LLM-Assisted DKG Evolution. The former retrieves question-relevant domain knowledge from DKG, uses it to prompt LLM for improved reasoning in domain-specific tasks, and the latter leverages LLM to generate new domain knowledge from processed tasks to evolve DKG. WTS closes the loop between these components, enabling continuous improvement in domain specialization as it progressively answers and learns from domain-specific questions. The performance of WTS was validated on 6 datasets spanning 5 domains, showing a maximum performance improvement of 11.3%.

title: As large language models (LLMs) generate increasingly human-like text, watermarking offers a promising solution for...
The exponential growth of large language models has outpaced the capabilities of traditional CPU and...
LLMs are increasingly being used to assess the relevance of information objects. This work reports...
categories: The methods used in this research include experiments to study the labelling of short texts (i.e., passages) for relevance using multiple open-source and proprietary LLMs. The experiments involve comparing the labels assigned by LLMs with human judgements. To further examine cases where human judges and LLMs disagree, the study injects query words into random and irrelevant passages. The results are analyzed to understand the influence of query words on LLM performance.

title: Large Language Model (LLM) conditioning refers to instructing an LLM to generate content in accordance...
categories: Zhyper is a parameter-efficient factorized hypernetwork framework that generates context-aware LoRA adapters from textual descriptions. The method aims to address the issue of conditioning Large Language Models (LLMs) by introducing fewer parameters compared to methods that directly condition LoRA weights.

title: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have raised interest in their formal reasoning capabilities,...
We present the Judge Reliability Harness, an open source library for constructing validation suites that...
abstract: The Judge Reliability Harness is an open source library designed to test the reliability of LLM judges. It evaluates both binary judgment accuracy and ordinal grading performance for various task formats. The evaluation reveals meaningful variation in performance across models and perturbation types, emphasizing opportunities to improve the robustness of LLM judges. Preliminary experiments on judges revealed consistency issues due to factors such as simple text formatting changes, paraphrasing, changes in verbosity, and flipping ground truth labels in LLM-produced responses.

title: Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback (RLAIF) has demonstrated significant potential across various domains, including mitigating...
categories: Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback (RLAIF) framework, Extracting AI feedback from a larger LLM (e.g., GPT-3.5) through a specialized prompting strategy, Training a reward model towards better alignment from smaller LLMs, Experiments on the Gorilla dataset, Assessment of the quality of the model-generated code across various metrics including AST, ROUGE, and Code-BLEU, Development of a pipeline to compute its executability rate accurately

title: We present Plancraft, a multi-modal evaluation dataset for LLM agents. Plancraft has both a text-only...
abstract: Plancraft is a multi-modal evaluation dataset for LLM agents, featuring both text-only and multi-modal interfaces based on Minecraft's crafting GUI. The dataset includes the Minecraft Wiki to evaluate tool use and Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG), as well as a handcrafted planner and Oracle Retriever for ablation of various agent components. It also contains a subset of unsolvable examples to test decision-making capabilities. The study finds that LLMs and VLMs struggle with the planning problems in Plancraft, suggesting potential improvements.

title: Understanding the uncertainty in large language model (LLM) explanations is important for evaluating their faithfulness...
abstract: This work focuses on quantifying uncertainty in large language model (LLM) explanations through a reasoning topology perspective. The proposed framework guides LLMs to frame explanations into a graph topology, allowing decomposition into related sub-questions and reasoning structures. By doing this, the method aims to quantify uncertainty at both semantic and reasoning path levels. It also facilitates assessment of knowledge redundancy and offers interpretable insights into the reasoning process.
categories: Designing a structural elicitation strategy to guide LLMs in framing explanations as graph topologies, Decomposing explanations into sub-questions and topology-based reasoning structures

title: Large Language Models (LLMs) can be fine-tuned on domain-specific data to enhance their performance in...
abstract: The abstract discusses LLM-AutoDP, a novel framework that uses Large Language Models (LLMs) to automatically generate and optimize data processing strategies. The goal is to achieve automated data processing without exposing raw data, especially in high-privacy domains like healthcare. The proposed method generates multiple candidate strategies, iteratively refines them using feedback signals and comparative evaluations, and employs an iterative in-context learning mechanism.
categories: Generation of multiple candidate data processing strategies using LLMs as agents, Iterative refinement of strategies using feedback signals and comparative evaluations, Incorporation of a Distribution Preserving Sampling technique to reduce data volume while maintaining distributional integrity, Implementation of Processing Target Selection, which uses a binary classifier to identify low-quality samples for focused processing, Use of Cache-and-Reuse Mechanism to minimize redundant computations by reusing prior processing results

title: Prompt injection threatens novel applications that emerge from adapting LLMs for various user tasks. The...
abstract: The abstract discusses the threat of prompt injection attacks on LLM-based software applications and focuses on detecting prompt leakage attacks, specifically. The study analyzes distinct prompt leakage detection techniques and compares several solutions that implement them, identifying their strengths and weaknesses. It also proposes improvements for the less effective techniques and offers a performance comparison of LLM Guard, Vigil, and Rebuff.
categories:
Investigation of capabilities of early prompt injection detection systems.
Analysis of distinct prompt leakage detection techniques.
Comparative analysis of several detection solutions implementing those techniques.
Identification of strengths and weaknesses of these techniques.
Elaboration on optimal configuration and usage in high-stake deployments.
Comparison of the performances of LLM Guard, Vigil, and Rebuff.

title: Large language models (LLMs) are used in a variety of mission-critical roles. Due to the...
abstract: This paper presents an approach to address the lack of understanding about bias and perspective in large language models (LLMs). The approach, called Perspective-Dial, includes a metric space (Perspective Space) to quantify different perspectives related to a topic and Systematic Prompt Engineering to control LLM output based on measurements from the Perspective Space. Potential applications include detecting, tracking, and mitigating LLM bias, understanding public discourse, and creating debate bots that advocate a specific perspective.

title: Content moderation is a global challenge, yet major tech platforms prioritize high-resource languages, leaving low-resource...
abstract: The abstract discusses the challenge of content moderation, particularly for low-resource languages due to a lack of native moderators. It highlights that non-native moderators struggle with interpreting culturally-specific knowledge, sentiment, and internet culture in hate speech moderation. To address this, a system called LLM-C3MOD is presented, which uses a human-LLM collaborative pipeline to improve cross-cultural hate speech moderation. The system was evaluated on a Korean hate speech dataset with Indonesian and German participants, achieving 78% accuracy (surpassing GPT-4o's 71% baseline) while reducing human workload by 83.6%. Human moderators were found to excel at nuanced contents where LLMs struggle.

title: Before adopting a new large language model (LLM) architecture, it is critical to understand vulnerabilities...
abstract: The paper introduces a principled and practical end-to-end framework for evaluating LLM vulnerabilities to prompt injection attacks. This includes an improved Bayesian hierarchical model with embedding-space clustering, designed to capture inherent uncertainty in non-deterministic LLM outputs and imperfect test prompts. The model's improved inferential capabilities are demonstrated across several prompt injection attack settings. Findings suggest that consideration of output variability can lead to less definitive findings, but for some attacks, Transformer and Mamba-variant vulnerabilities are notably increased across LLMs with the same training data or mathematical ability.

title: Large language models (LLMs) excel in open domains but struggle in specialized settings with limited...
abstract: The abstract introduces AutoAdapt, a novel end-to-end automated framework for efficient and reliable Large Language Model (LLM) domain adaptation. The goal is to improve LLM performance in specialized settings with limited data and evolving knowledge. AutoAdapt leverages curated knowledge bases from literature and open-source resources to reduce expert intervention. A novel multi-agent debating system is designed for iterative proposal and critic agent interactions, aligning user intent and incorporating data signals and best practices.
categories: AutoAdapt employs a LLM-based surrogate (AutoRefine) to optimize hyperparameters under tight budgets, replacing costly black-box search., The multi-agent debating system includes proposal and critic agents that iteratively interact to align user intent and incorporate data signals and best practices into the planning process.

title: Deciding which large language model (LLM) to use is a complex challenge. Pairwise ranking has...
abstract: The paper discusses the complex challenge of choosing a Large Language Model (LLM). A new method, pairwise ranking, is proposed for evaluating human preferences for LLMs. This involves humans comparing model outputs based on predefined criteria, and using algorithms like Elo to construct rankings. However, applying these algorithms in the context of LLM evaluation presents challenges. The paper investigates the effectiveness of ranking systems for head-to-head comparisons of LLMs. It defines fundamental principles for effective ranking and conducts extensive evaluations on the robustness of several ranking algorithms in the context of LLMs. The analysis reveals key insights into factors affecting ranking accuracy and efficiency, providing guidelines for selecting suitable methods based on specific evaluation contexts and resource constraints.

categories: CUPID, Evaluation of 10 open and proprietary LLMs using a benchmark of 756 human-curated interaction session histories between users and LLM-based chat assistants. In each interaction session, the user provides a request in a specific context and expresses their preference through multi-turn feedback. Given a new user request and prior interaction sessions, the benchmark assesses whether LLMs can infer the preference relevant to this request and generate a response that satisfies this preference.

abstract: The abstract discusses a new PyTorch-native framework called Modalities for large-scale language model (LLM) research and training. The primary goal is to improve efficiency in pretraining and ablation studies, particularly at trillion-token and billion-parameter scales. Modalities integrates advanced parallelization strategies and offers a modular design with declarative configuration for enhanced reproducibility and extensibility.

title: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in medicine. However, their utility in non-generative clinical...
abstract: The study benchmarks various Large Language Models (LLMs) and traditional methods on unstructured clinical notes and structured Electronic Health Records (EHR). Key findings reveal that leading LLMs, such as DeepSeek-V3.1-Think and GPT-5, outperform finetuned BERT models in zero-shot settings for clinical note predictions. On structured EHRs, advanced LLMs demonstrate potent zero-shot capabilities, often surpassing conventional models in data-scarce settings. Notably, open-source LLMs can match or exceed proprietary counterparts. The results suggest that modern LLMs are competitive tools for non-generative clinical prediction. These findings provide compelling evidence that modern LLMs are valuable tools in predictive healthcare and may necessitate a re-evaluation of model selection strategies.
categories: The study compares 15 GPT-style LLMs, 5 BERT-style models, and 11 traditional methods., The assessment includes evaluations of their reasoning, reliability, and fairness., Data used for benchmarking comes from unstructured clinical notes and structured Electronic Health Records (EHR).

The research introduces LinkQ, a KG exploration system that converts natural language questions into structured queries using an LLM.

title: Mitigating Trojans in Large Language Models (LLMs) is one of many tasks where alignment data...
abstract: The paper presents TeleLoRA, a novel framework for mitigating Trojans in Large Language Models (LLMs). It synergizes model-specific alignment data across multiple LLMs to enable zero-shot Trojan mitigation on unseen LLMs. The generator of LoRA adapter weights is learned using local activation information and designed to be permutation symmetric for generalization. The model design is optimized for memory efficiency, making it feasible for large-scale LLMs with minimal computational resources. Experiments demonstrate that TeleLoRA effectively reduces attack success rates while preserving the benign performance of models.

categories: This study employs cumulative prospect theory (CPT) and the Big Five personality framework to investigate LLMs' behavior under risk. The research measures the personality traits of GPT-4o using the Big Five model, which includes Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, and Neuroticism. Interventions on GPT-4o's Big Five traits are performed to analyze their impact on risk propensity. Comparisons are made between GPT-4o, human baselines, and earlier models.

The questions require special attention in answering and cover a wide range of risk categories established in prior English-language datasets. The data samples are original as they are manually created to reflect the socio-cultural context of LLM usage in Japan. The dataset is used for instruction to fine-tune a Japanese LLM, which led to improved output safety without compromising the utility of general responses.

abstract: The study reveals that small LLMs can run on mobile devices but with quality limitations compared to larger models. Model compression can reduce hardware requirements but may lead to performance degradation. Latency for running LLMs on mobile devices is significant (>30 seconds), while cloud services demonstrate better time efficiency (<10 seconds). Edge deployments offer intermediate tradeoffs between latency and model capabilities, depending on the CPU or GPU setting. These findings offer valuable insights for system designers regarding the current limitations and future directions for on-device LLM applications.

title: Deploying advanced large language models on edge devices, such as smartphones and robotics, is a...
abstract: The study introduces Cambricon-LLM, a chiplet-based hybrid architecture designed for efficient on-device inference of large language models (LLMs) on edge devices like smartphones and robotics. This architecture combines a Neural Processing Unit (NPU) and a dedicated NAND flash chip, utilizing both their high computing capability and data capacity respectively. A hardware-tiling strategy minimizes the data movement overhead between NPU and NAND flash chip. The innovative in-flash computing and on-die ECC techniques enhance the NAND flash chip's ability to perform precise lightweight on-die processing. The NPU collaborates with the flash chip for matrix operations and handles special function computations beyond the flash's on-die processing capabilities. This hybrid architecture enables the on-device inference of 70B LLMs at a speed of 3.44 token/s, and 7B LLMs at a speed of 36.34 token/s.

abstract: The abstract discusses the use of large language models (LLMs) in social science simulations, focusing on their ability to mimic human decision-making variability and adaptability. A process-oriented evaluation framework is proposed with interventions such as Intrinsicality, Instruction, and Imitation to examine LLM agent adaptation under varying levels of guidance and human-derived noise. The study shows that by default, LLMs converge on conservative strategies that differ from observed human behaviors. Risk-framed instructions affect LLM behavior but do not replicate human diversity. Using human data through in-context learning reduces the gap but fails to match human subjects' strategic variability. The research highlights a persistent alignment gap and suggests future evaluations should focus on process-level realism for LLMs in dynamic decision-making tasks, offering guidance for their application in synthetic data for social science research.

title: Recent development in large language models (LLMs) has demonstrated impressive domain proficiency on unstructured textual...
abstract: This paper proposes SERSAL, a self-prompting method that leverages synergy learning with small models to enhance large language models (LLMs) in predicting structured tabular data in an unsupervised manner. SERSAL uses the LLM's prior outcomes as original soft noisy annotations to train a better small student model, and vice versa. This process is repeated for continuous knowledge refinement. Experiments on medical domain tabular datasets show that SERSAL improves LLM reasoning significantly compared to linguistic prompting methods, serving as a promising direction for tabular LLMs, especially with the emergence of more powerful models.

abstract: The study compares the performance of audited OLMo-2 models with unaudited open-weight and proprietary baselines. The research focuses on their abilities to perform 'critical confabulation', a process of filling in gaps in historical archives for underrepresented figures, using LLMs (Large Language Models) to generate plausible yet evidence-bound narratives. Findings validate the foundational narrative understanding capabilities of LLMs and demonstrate how controlled hallucinations can support knowledge production without compromising historical accuracy and fidelity.

The text highlights that these accelerators, while adopting multi-core architectures for enhanced scalability, lack the flexibility of SIMT architectures. Underutilization of computational resources due to improper hardware architecture configuration and design can lead to suboptimal inference performance.

The integration aims to combine localized structural learning with global chemical knowledge, achieved by concatenating LLM embeddings after each GCN layer.

abstract: The abstract discusses the potential of large language models (LLMs) in creating pedagogical agents for student learning, particularly focused on STEM+C subjects. The proposed framework combines Evidence-Centered Design with Social Cognitive Theory to enhance adaptive scaffolding in LLM-based agents. The abstract presents Inquizzitor, an example of this LLM-based agent that offers formative assessment and provides feedback grounded in cognitive science principles.

title: Jailbreaks on large language models (LLMs) have recently received increasing attention. For a comprehensive assessment...
abstract: The paper discusses the issue of jailbreaks on large language models (LLMs) and the need for a comprehensive assessment of LLM safety. It proposes a novel connection between controllable attack generation and controllable text generation, leading to the introduction of the COLD-Attack framework which automates the search of adversarial LLM attacks under various control requirements such as fluency, stealthiness, sentiment, and left-right coherence. The framework is shown to be broadly applicable, highly controllable, and successful on multiple LLMs. The COLD-Attack framework adapts the Energy-based Constrained Decoding with Langevin Dynamics (COLD), a state-of-the-art algorithm in controllable text generation, to generate diverse new jailbreak scenarios beyond just generating fluent suffix attacks. These new settings include revising user queries adversarially with paraphrasing constraints and inserting stealthy attacks in context with position constraints. The paper presents experiments on various LLMs (Llama-2, Mistral, Vicuna, Guanaco, GPT-3.5, and GPT-4) demonstrating the broad applicability, strong controllability, high success rate, and attack transferability of COLD-Attack.
categories: Formal formulation of the controllable attack generation problem, Adaptation of Energy-based Constrained Decoding with Langevin Dynamics (COLD), Introduction of the COLD-Attack framework which unifies and automates the search of adversarial LLM attacks under various control requirements such as fluency, stealthiness, sentiment, and left-right coherence

title: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive performances in tasks related to coreference resolution. However,...
abstract: This study evaluates the performance of Large Language Models (LLMs) on coreference resolution with indexicals like 'I', 'you', 'here', and 'tomorrow'. It presents the English Indexical Dataset containing 1600 multiple-choice questions for this analysis. The study compares the performance of pioneering LLMs, including GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 1.5 Pro, and DeepSeek V3. The results show that while LLMs exhibit an impressive performance with some indexicals (I), they struggle with others (you, here, tomorrow). Syntactic cues play a role in the performance of LLMs with certain indexicals, but negatively impact performance with others. The study reveals that large language models have demonstrated strong capabilities in coreference resolution tasks involving nouns and third person pronouns, but this work focuses on indexicals which present unique challenges due to their linguistic properties.
categories: The study evaluates LLMs performance using the English Indexical Dataset containing 1600 multiple-choice questions., Four pioneering LLMs, GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 1.5 Pro, and DeepSeek V3, are compared in this study.

abstract: The paper systematically evaluates Large Language Models (LLMs) behavior on long-tail distributed encrypted texts and their safety implications. It introduces a two-dimensional framework for assessing LLM safety: instruction refusal-the ability to reject harmful obfuscated instructions, and generation safety-the suppression of generating harmful responses. Through experiments, it is shown that models capable of decrypting ciphers may be vulnerable to mismatched-generalization attacks, causing failures in safety mechanisms leading to unsafe responses or over-refusal.
categories: Comprehensive experiments were conducted to demonstrate the vulnerabilities of LLMs., The evaluation involved assessing LLM safety using a two-dimensional framework: instruction refusal and generation safety., Mismatched-generalization attacks were applied to test the susceptibility of models.

title: LLM (large language model) practitioners commonly notice that outputs can vary for the same inputs...
abstract: The abstract discusses the issue of non-determinism in large language models (LLMs), revealing that outputs can vary for the same inputs even under deterministic settings. This study investigates this phenomenon in five LLMs across eight common tasks, over ten runs, both in zero-shot and few-shot settings. The results show accuracy variations up to 15%, with a gap of best possible performance to worst possible performance reaching 70%. The authors conclude that none of the LLMs deliver repeatable accuracy across all tasks or identical output strings. They also suggest that non-determinism might be essential for efficient use of compute resources due to co-mingled data in input buffers, implying this issue persists. To better quantify their observations, they introduce new metrics: TARr@N for total agreement rate at N runs over raw output, and TARa@N for total agreement rate of parsed-out answers. The researchers have made their code and data publicly available.

abstract: The study found no significant difference in overall feedback quality between LLM-generated feedback and feedback provided by teachers and science education experts. However, the LLM agent's performance lagged in the 'Feed Back' dimension, which involves identifying and explaining errors within a student's work context. Qualitative analysis highlighted the LLM agent's limitations in contextual understanding and clear communication of specific errors. The findings suggest that combining LLM-generated feedback with human expertise could enhance educational practices by leveraging the efficiency of LLMs and the nuanced understanding of educators.

title: Evaluating the conversational abilities of large language models (LLMs) remains a challenging task. Current mainstream...
abstract: The paper discusses the challenges in evaluating conversational abilities of large language models (LLMs). It criticizes the common 'LLM-as-a-judge' paradigm for its biases and high computational costs. The proposed solution is an efficient dialogue evaluator that aggregates preference knowledge from multiple LLM judges into a single model, preserving diverse feedback while reducing evaluation cost. The method offers fast, flexible, and fine-grained dialogue quality assessment on seven single rating and pairwise comparison dialogue evaluation benchmarks, outperforming existing baselines across various scenarios.

abstract: The abstract discusses the instability of leading Large Language Models (LLMs) like gpt-4o, claude-3.5, and gemini-1.5 when providing answers to complex legal questions, even with deterministic settings. A novel dataset of 500 real-case legal questions is introduced, revealing that these LLMs sometimes contradict themselves when asked the same question, stating different parties should prevail. This inconsistency may have significant implications for the growing number of legal AI products, processes, and lawyers relying on such models.
